function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC

The **FY2025 Space segment revenue** is **$4,086 million** for the **year ended December 31, 2025**.

For comparison, the prior year (FY2024) Space segment revenue was **$3,796 million**.

This represents a year-over-year increase of **$290 million**, or **7.6%**, as detailed in the Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) section.

Based on the provided text excerpts from a SpaceX prospectus preamble, here is a comprehensive summary of all disclosed mass-to-orbit data, launch volumes, payload capacities, and related KPIs.

### 1. Mass to Orbit (Key Performance Indicator)
**Definition:** Total kilograms of payload deployed to orbit in a given period, including Starlink satellites, customer payloads, and development cargo. Excludes failed or scrubbed attempts.

**Historical Annual Data:**
*   **2023:** 1,210 metric tons
*   **2024:** 1,699 metric tons
*   **2025:** 2,213 metric tons (representing **>80% of global mass to orbit** for the year)

**Historical Quarterly Data:**
*   **Q1 2025:** 450 metric tons
*   **Q1 2026:** 556 metric tons

**Cumulative Total (as of March 31, 2026):** Approximately **7,400 metric tons** launched across all Falcon rockets.

**Breakdown by Payload Source (Annual):**
| Year | Customer Payloads (metric tons) | Internal Payloads (metric tons) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2023 | 205 | 1,005 |
| 2024 | 282 | 1,418 |
| 2025 | 312 | 1,901 |
*(Note: Totals may not sum due to rounding)*

### 2. Launch Volume & Cadence
**Key Business Metric:** Measures operational scale across successful orbital/flight tests.

**Historical Annual Data:**
*   **2023:** 98 launches
*   **2024:** 138 launches
*   **2025:** 170 launches (165 Falcon 9, 5 Starship flights)

**Historical Quarterly Data:**
*   **Q1 2025:** 38 launches
*   **Q1 2026:** 40 launches

**Cumulative Totals (as of March 31, 2026):**
*   **~650** total orbital space launches.
*   **~620** Falcon 9 orbital launches.
*   **11** Starship flight tests completed.

**Flight-Proven Booster Reuse (2025):**
*   159 flight-proven booster launches out of 170 total missions.

### 3. Payload Mass Capacities per Vehicle
**Falcon 9:**
*   **LEO Capacity:** ~23 metric tons.
*   **GTO Capacity:** ~8 metric tons.
*   **Average Capacity per Mission:** 13 metric tons (since 2023).

**Falcon Heavy:**
*   **LEO Capacity:** ~64 metric tons.
*   **GTO Capacity:** ~27 metric tons.

**Starship (Next-Generation System):**
*   **V3 Design Capacity:** **100 metric tons** to LEO.
*   **Future (V4) Design Capacity:** **200 metric tons** to LEO.
*   **Starship Payload Bay Volume:** Comparable to the pressurized sections of the International Space Station.

**Dragon Cargo Spacecraft:**
*   **Launch Payload Mass:** Up to 6,000 kg.
*   **Return Payload Mass:** 3,000 kg.

### 4. Market Context & Cost Metrics
**Global Market Share:**
*   In 2025, SpaceX launched **>80%** of the world's mass to orbit.
*   SpaceX owns **~75%** of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit (as of March 31, 2026).

**Launch Cost Reduction (per kg to orbit):**
*   **Historical Average:** $18,500/kg.
*   **Falcon 9 (2010):** ~$2,700/kg (an **85% reduction**).
*   **Falcon Heavy (2018):** ~$1,400/kg (a **92% reduction**).

### 5. Future Projections & Strategic Goals
**Orbital AI Compute Deployment:**
*   Long-term goal: Deploy **up to 100 gigawatts of power** to orbit annually.
*   This would require transporting **~1,000,000 metric tons** to orbit annually, assuming 100 kW compute power per metric ton and Starship's 100-ton capacity.
*   A more commercially viable starting point is a **10 gigawatt annual deployment rate**.

**Lunar Economy & Mars:**
*   Starship's design supports transporting hundreds of metric tons of cargo to destinations like the Moon and Mars.
*   A petawatt-scale AI constellation is envisioned through lunar satellite production and a lunar mass driver.

Based on the provided text excerpts, here are the answers to your questions:

### 1. Revenue Allocation Between Customer Launches vs. Internal/Starlink Launches

The disclosures clearly distinguish between customer launches and internal launches, and state that **only customer launches generate revenue for the Space segment.**

*   **Customer Launch Definition:** A launch is designated as a "customer launch" if an external customer payload is the primary payload and the mission parameters are designed around that payload's requirements. **All Starship launches to date are classified as internal.**
*   **Revenue Recognition:** "Our Space segment revenue only reflects our customer launches and customer activities." (Repeated in multiple sections).
*   **Financial Impact:** "Notwithstanding an increasing launch cadence, our Space segment has relatively lower revenue scale and revenue growth compared to our other segments," because a significant and growing portion of launch capacity is allocated internally.
*   **Historical Data:** For Falcon launches in 2023, 2024, and 2025, customer launches represented 33/96, 45/134, and 43/165 of total launches, respectively. Internal launches (including Starlink satellite deployments) represented the majority.

### 2. Intersegment Revenue or Transfer Pricing for Starlink Launches

The documents explicitly state there is **no intersegment revenue** recognized for Starlink satellite launches. Instead, the costs are capitalized.

*   **Key Statement:** "For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in *Property, plant, and equipment, net*." This is reiterated in multiple sections.
*   **Transfer Pricing Implication:** The accounting treatment described ("capitalize the associated costs within our Connectivity segment and depreciate them over time") effectively operates as a cost-sharing or transfer pricing mechanism without generating recognizable revenue for the Space segment. The strategic value is in enabling the Connectivity segment's growth, not in inter-segment sales.

### 3. Discussion of Cost Per Launch or Cost Efficiency Metrics

While specific "cost per launch" figures are not disclosed, the documents discuss **cost efficiency drivers and trends.**

*   **Primary Efficiency Driver:** **Reusability.** "Our Space Segment Adjusted EBITDA is also driven by the reusability and efficiency of our rockets, which boosts cadence and reliability..."
*   **Financial Impact of Reusability:**
    *   For 2024, a decrease in Space segment cost of revenue was "primarily due to increased reusability of our Falcon launch vehicles resulting in lower depreciation of $80 million, lowering the cost of each launch."
    *   Increased reuse leads to lower depreciation per flight.
*   **Future Expectation:** Efficiency is expected to improve further with Starship: "Starship is designed to enable a step-function change in our launch capability across reusability, payload capacity, and launch cadence..."
*   **Segment-Level Metric:** The documents focus on segment-level profitability (Adjusted EBITDA) rather than per-unit launch economics. The Space segment's financial results are shown to be influenced by the mix of launches (customer vs. internal) and heavy R&D investment in Starship.

**Summary:** The financial disclosures frame the Space segment's role as foundational but internally focused. Its revenue comes only from external customers, it does not bill the Connectivity segment for Starlink launches (costs are capitalized instead), and its cost efficiency is primarily driven by rocket reusability, though specific per-launch cost data is not provided.

Based on the provided excerpts from the MD&A section, here is the disclosure regarding the Space segment's revenue breakdown and customer launch activity for FY2025.

### **Breakdown of Space Segment Revenue**
The company's Space segment revenue is broken down into two primary categories: **Launch Services** and **Launch & Development**.

*   **Launch Services:** Revenue from fixed-price contracts for the deployment of payloads to their intended orbits using Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy. This revenue is recognized at a **point in time** upon the launch or deployment.
*   **Launch & Development:** Revenue from the development of spacecraft and provision of launch and mission services for government agency space programs using Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship, and **Dragon**. This revenue is recognized **over time** using the cost-to-cost input method. # SpaceX Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Analysis and Legacy Provider Comparison

## 1. Key Filing Data Extracted

| Metric | FY2025 Value | Source |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Space Segment Revenue | $4,086M | MD&A segment table |
| Launch Services Revenue (63% of total) | $2,574M | Revenue recognition policy table |
| Launch & Development Revenue (37%) | $1,512M | Revenue recognition policy table |
| Customer Launches (Falcon) | 43 | KPI table |
| Internal Launches (Falcon) | 122 | KPI table |
| Total Falcon Launches | 165 | KPI table |
| Customer Mass to Orbit | 312 metric tons | KPI table |
| Internal Mass to Orbit | 1,901 metric tons | KPI table |
| Total Mass to Orbit | 2,213 metric tons | KPI table |
| Space Segment Cost of Revenue | $1,352M | Segment P&L |
| Space Segment Gross Profit (derived) | $2,734M | Revenue minus cost of revenue |
| Space Segment Gross Margin (derived) | 66.9% | Gross profit / revenue |

**Critical accounting note:** The filing explicitly states that Starlink satellite launch costs are **not** expensed through Space segment cost of revenue; instead, they are **capitalized** within the Connectivity segment's property, plant and equipment. Therefore, Space segment cost of revenue relates to **customer launches only**, making the gross margin calculation economically meaningful for this proxy exercise.

---

## 2. Implied Revenue-per-Kilogram Proxy Calculations

### Proxy A: Total Space Revenue / Customer Mass to Orbit
$$\frac{\$4{,}086\text{M}}{312{,}000\text{ kg}} = \$13{,}096 \text{ per kg}$$

This is the broadest proxy. It includes Launch & Development revenue (NASA CRS, DoW contracts, Dragon mission services) alongside pure launch revenue, and divides by the mass of customer payloads deployed. It **overstates** the pure per-kg launch price because ~37% of revenue comes from development contracts priced on a cost-to-cost basis, not per-kilogram.

### Proxy B: Launch Services Revenue / Customer Mass to Orbit
$$\frac{\$2{,}574\text{M}}{312{,}000\text{ kg}} = \$8{,}251 \text{ per kg}$$

This isolates the 63% of revenue recognized at point-in-time upon payload deployment (the closest analog to a per-launch/per-kg price). It is the **more conservative and economically representative** proxy for pure launch pricing.

### Proxy C: Cost of Revenue / Customer Mass to Orbit
$$\frac{\$1{,}352\text{M}}{312{,}000\text{ kg}} = \$4{,}333 \text{ per kg}$$

This represents the implied cost to SpaceX of delivering one customer kilogram to orbit (inclusive of booster depreciation, propellant, range costs, mission assurance, etc.).

### Proxy D: Gross Profit / Customer Mass to Orbit
$$\frac{\$2{,}734\text{M}}{312{,}000\text{ kg}} = \$8{,}763 \text{ per kg}$$

### Proxy E: Average Revenue per Customer Launch
$$\frac{\$4{,}086\text{M}}{43} = \$95.0\text{M per launch}$$

The filing notes that average price per launch remained "relatively flat year over year." The $95M blended average exceeds the widely cited $67M Falcon 9 list price because it includes higher-value Falcon Heavy missions, premium-priced government development contracts, and Dragon CRS missions.

### Average Customer Payload per Mission
$$\frac{312{,}000\text{ kg}}{43} = 7{,}256 \text{ kg per launch}$$

This is well below Falcon 9's ~22,800 kg LEO capacity, indicating most customer missions operate at roughly **one-third of maximum payload capacity** — a structural factor that inflates per-kg pricing above theoretical list-price minimums.

---

## 3. Directional Comparison to Legacy Launch Providers

| Provider / Vehicle | Estimated Price per Launch | LEO Capacity | Estimated $/kg to LEO | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SpaceX Falcon 9 (list price)** | ~$67M | ~22,800 kg | ~$2,940 | SpaceX published pricing |
| **SpaceX implied (Launch Services / customer kg)** | — | — | **~$8,251** | Derived from S-1 |
| **SpaceX implied (total revenue / customer kg)** | — | — | **~$13,096** | Derived from S-1 |
| **ULA Vulcan Centaur** | ~$100–150M | ~27,200 kg | ~$5,500–$9,800 | Industry estimates |
| **Arianespace Ariane 62** | ~$77–85M | ~10,350 kg | ~$7,400–$8,200 | Industry estimates |
| **Arianespace Ariane 64** | ~$90–115M | ~21,650 kg | ~$5,300–$10,000 | Industry estimates |
| **Historical industry average** | — | — | ~$18,500 | Filing disclosure (preamble) |

### Key Observations:

1. **The $2,940/kg Falcon 9 list price is a theoretical floor**, not an achieved average. The filing's implied $8,251–$13,096/kg range is far higher because customer missions on average deploy only ~7,256 kg per launch (one-third of max LEO capacity), and many missions target GTO or specialized orbits with lower payload mass.

2. **SpaceX's implied per-kg pricing sits in the middle of the legacy provider range** ($5,300–$10,000/kg for Ariane 6 and Vulcan), but this comparison is misleading. SpaceX's figure is inflated by the inclusion of government development contracts (Launch & Development revenue) and Dragon mission services, which are priced on milestones and cost-plus basis, not per-kg. The true per-kg pricing for pure commercial launches is likely closer to the $2,940–$5,500/kg range.

3. **Even at the blended implied rate, SpaceX is ~30–40% below the historical industry average** of $18,500/kg cited in the filing, demonstrating the cost disruption that reusability has enabled.

4. **SpaceX's gross margin of 66.9%** on the Space segment suggests significant pricing power — the company is not simply undercutting competitors on price but is doing so while generating substantial unit-level profitability. This is consistent with a near-monopoly position (>80% of global mass to orbit in 2025).

---

## 4. What the Comparison Suggests About Pricing Power and Margin Structure

**Pricing Power:**
- SpaceX's dominant market share (>80% of global mass to orbit) gives it pricing power that legacy providers cannot match. The company can price below competitors while still earning a 66.9% gross margin.
- The filing's disclosure that "average price per launch remained relatively flat year over year" despite increasing cadence suggests SpaceX is not aggressively cutting prices — it has no competitive incentive to do so given its market dominance.
- Government contracts (Launch & Development) likely carry premium pricing, further boosting the blended per-kg figure.

**Margin Structure:**
- The $4,333/kg implied cost of revenue represents the all-in variable cost per customer kilogram delivered, including booster depreciation (reduced by reuse — the filing notes $80M in lower depreciation from increased reuse in FY2024), propellant, operations, and mission assurance.
- The $8,763/kg implied gross profit demonstrates that SpaceX earns roughly **$2 of gross profit for every $1 of cost** on customer launches.
- Starship development costs ($3,004M in R&D for FY2025) drive the segment to an operating loss, but this is an intentional investment phase, not a structural margin problem. Management explicitly states R&D will "moderate" after Starship commercialization.

**Structural Advantage:**
- The 159/165 Falcon 9 booster reuse rate in 2025 (96%) is the primary margin driver. Each booster flies multiple times, amortizing the ~$30M manufacturing cost across many missions and reducing effective per-launch depreciation.
- Vertical integration (Merlin/Raptor engines, Dragon spacecraft, fairings) gives SpaceX cost control that no competitor matches.

---

## 5. The Disclosure Gap That Prevents Precise Margin-per-Kilogram Calculation

The filing contains **five critical disclosure gaps** that prevent a precise margin-per-kg calculation:

### Gap 1: No Per-Launch Cost Breakdown
The filing does not disclose cost of revenue on a per-launch, per-vehicle, or per-mission basis. The $1,352M in Space segment cost of revenue is reported only in aggregate. Without knowing the cost of an individual Falcon 9 launch vs. a Falcon Heavy launch vs. a Dragon CRS mission, the per-kg cost figure ($4,333/kg) is a blended average that masks significant cost heterogeneity.

### Gap 2: No Revenue Decomposition by Mission Type
While the filing splits revenue into "Launch Services" (63%) and "Launch & Development" (37%), it does not further decompose these categories. The 43 customer launches include a mix of commercial launches, government national security missions, NASA CRS resupply flights, and development milestones — each with fundamentally different pricing structures (fixed-price per launch vs. cost-to-cost progress payments). The implied $13,096/kg total revenue proxy conflates these distinct revenue models.

### Gap 3: No Orbit-Specific Pricing or Mass Allocation
The 312 metric tons of customer mass-to-orbit aggregates all orbits (LEO, GTO, SSO, MEO, etc.). GTO missions carry ~8,000 kg on Falcon 9 vs. ~22,800 kg to LEO, and command significantly higher per-kg pricing due to greater energy requirements. Without orbit-specific mass and revenue data, the per-kg proxy is a weighted average across orbits with very different economics.

### Gap 4: Internal Launch Cost Capitalization Obscures True Launch Economics
Starlink launch costs are capitalized as satellite assets rather than expensed through cost of revenue. This means we cannot observe the full cost curve of a Falcon 9 launch — only the cost attributable to customer missions. The capitalized internal launch costs are embedded in the Connectivity segment's depreciation schedule, making it impossible to back out the marginal cost of an incremental launch or to compare SpaceX's full-cost launch economics against competitors.

### Gap 5: No Average Selling Price (ASP) Disclosure per Customer Launch
The filing states that "average price per launch remained relatively flat" but does not disclose the actual ASP. The derived $95.0M average ($4,086M / 43 launches) includes non-launch revenue streams (development contracts, Dragon services), making it an overstatement of the pure commercial launch ASP. A precise per-kg margin calculation requires knowing the actual launch price charged for each mission, which is not disclosed.

---

## 6. Summary Table

| Metric | Value | Limitation |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Implied revenue/customer kg (total) | $13,096/kg | Includes development contract revenue |
| Implied revenue/customer kg (Launch Services) | $8,251/kg | Best proxy but still blended across orbits/missions |
| Implied cost/customer kg | $4,333/kg | Blended; no per-mission cost disclosure |
| Implied gross profit/customer kg | $8,763/kg | Embedded development revenue inflates numerator |
| Space segment gross margin | 66.9% | Cleanest available margin metric |
| Falcon 9 theoretical list price/kg (max LEO) | ~$2,940/kg | Assumes full payload — most missions use ~33% |
| Legacy provider range | $5,300–$10,000/kg | Public estimates, not audited |

**Bottom line:** The filing provides sufficient data to construct a directional revenue-per-kg proxy ($8,251–$13,096/kg) and to confirm that SpaceX operates with substantial gross margins (66.9%) well above legacy competitors. However, the absence of per-launch cost data, mission-level revenue decomposition, and orbit-specific mass allocation prevents investors from calculating a precise margin-per-kilogram for any individual mission type — a gap that is analytically material given the heterogeneity of SpaceX's customer manifest.
